content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D1.1: Deliverable D1.1: Use cases, requirements, performance evaluation criteria
content.abstract_or_snippet: attributes as part of the V2V Service). The V2V payload must be flexible in order to accommodate different information contents. V2V is predominantly broadcast-based. Where a direct communication might not be possible e.g. due to the limited direct communication range, the V2V related application information exchange might be done using other V2X services alternatively, e.g. using additional infrastructure like RSU or application servers and the different underlying communication paths of V2I, V2N etc. potentially in a combined manner. Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) The V2I communication will also be implemented using LTE Sidelink Transmission Mode 4 on PC5, i.e. the RSU acts similar as a UE. Note that the 3GPP standard also foresees a variant of a V2I communication via Uu link, then assuming the RSU to be part of an eNode B, but this setup is again not used within this trial as it is no probable option for a future real world implementation. The UE supporting V2I applications transmits messages containing V2I application information to an RSU. The RSU and/or the locally relevant application server transmit messages containing V2I application information to one or more UEs supporting V2I applications. A locally relevant application server serves a particular geographic area. There can be multiple application servers serving overlapping areas, providing the same or different applications. Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P) V2P communication is very similar to V2V, also using PC5 LTE Sidelink Transmission Mode 4. The UE supporting V2P applications transmits application layer information. Such information can be broadcast by a vehicle with UE supporting V2X Service (e.g., warning to pedestrian), and/or by a pedestrian with UE supporting V2X Service (e.g., warning to vehicle). ConVeX Project Confidential 14 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1 Vehicle to Network (V2N) V2N is also introduced where one party is a UE and the other party is a serving entity, supporting both V2N applications and communicating with each other via cellular network (e.g. LTE or 5G). This means for the LTE case, the Uu interface will be used. Network to Vehicle/UE communication can be multicast or unicast. The serving entities are usually connected to public internet, and possibly to the backends of several

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D1.1: Deliverable D1.1: Use cases, requirements, performance evaluation criteria
content.abstract_or_snippet: 1 ConVeX High Level Network Architecture Communication connections in the ConVeX system architecture can be classified in the following way: Direct communication such as vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) is realized through PC5 (ProSe Communication reference point 5). The same holds true for vehicle-to-pedestrians (V2P) and vehicle- to-infrastructure (V2I). In addition the LTE-Uu connection complements any communication in the non-direct links within the (wide area) network. This connection will also be used as backhaul-link between road-side-units (RSU) and its (traffic management) center, acting as transparent IP connection. Figure 4.2-1 ConVeX High Level Network Architecture Types of V2X The vehicular communication referred to as Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X), contains following four different types:  Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communications ConVeX Project Confidential 13 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1  Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) Communications  Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P) Communications  Vehicle-to-Network (V2N) Figure 4.2-1. illustrates these types of communications. Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) V2V communication is realized with the means of LTE V2V Sidelink on PC5 interface enabling a direct vehicle to vehicle communication. The LTE standard allows two different transmission modes on PC5: the so-called “in-coverage” mode, where an eNode B is scheduling the actual transmissions of the UE (Sidelink Transmission Mode 3), as well as the “out-of-coverage” mode where there is no eNode B scheduling involved, but an autonomous resource selection performed by the UEs (Sidelink Transmission Mode 4). The latter will be used for the trial and is also envisioned as the most relevant mode of operation for future real world usage. As mentioned above, the same PC5 communication will be used for V2I and V2P. The UE supporting V2V applications transmits application layer information (e.g. about its location, dynamics, and attributes as part of the V2V Service). The V2V payload must be flexible in order to accommodate different information contents. V2V is predominantly broadcast-based. Where a direct communication might not be possible e.g. due to the limited direct

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D4.1: Deliverable D4.1: Roadside ITS station specification
content.abstract_or_snippet: Service: A type of V2X Service, where one party is a UE and the other party is an RSU both using V2I application. V2N Service: A type of V2X Service, where one party is a UE and the other party is a serving entity, both using V2N applications and communicating with each other via cellular network (e.g. LTE or 5G) . V2P Service: A type of V2X Service, where both parties of the communication are UEs using V2P application. For this service the vehicle-side UE is a V-ITS-S, the pedestrian-side (respectively VRU-side) UE is a P-ITS-S. V2V Service: A type of V2X Service, where both parties of the communication are UEs using V2V application. For this service both vehicle-side UEs represent V-ITS-S. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D2.2: Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX system architecture description
content.abstract_or_snippet: this trial as it is no probable option for a future real world implementation. ConVeX Project 12 (76) Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX System Architecture Description Version: V1.0 Description of System Components C-V2X Communication Platform Overview The C-V2X Communication Platform can be used for car and roadside unit integration and supports advanced use cases for V2V, V2I, V2P and V2N. It is supposed to be used for development and testing of trial specific use cases. The main hardware component for the trial system is the C-V2X Communication Platform. Figure 5.1-1 shows a simplified structural and functional overview of the prototype implementation with its corresponding components. It is composed of the C-V2X Development Platform (C-V2X DP) shown on the right side linked via an Ethernet connection to the Communication Module (CCARD - Connected Car Application Reference Design) shown on the left side. In the trial setup, the CCARD is used for the V2N communication via an LTE network. The C-V2X Development Platform consists of the Application Processor (AP) module and the C- V2X Module and peripheral components. These two modules are linked and communicate via an Ethernet connection. The C-V2X DP furthermore provides external connectivity via Ethernet, CAN and USB. Figure 5.1-1: C-V2X Communication Platform (simplified) Architecture The AP module of the C-V2X Development Platform consists of a Qualcomm Snapdragon APQ8096 which runs the software providing the lower layer services, the ITS Stack and the individual V2X applications. Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) are provided to enable the usage of the different services. The figure shows the two most important APIs giving access to the ITS Facility layer as well as the lower entry point to e.g. use the modem functionalities. In addition, the AP also runs the security management functions which will ensure proper handling of the security credentials. The APQ8096 verifies ITS message security, evaluates position ConVeX Project 13 (76) 5.9 GHz(V2V/V2I/V2P)C-V2X Communication PlatformGNSSLTECommunication Module(CCARD)(LTE Network Connectivity Only)700/2600 MHz(V2N)Data ServiceEthernetEthernetCANUSBV2X ApplicationsFacility serviceSecurity serviceModem/GNSS/CAN servicesApplication Processor (AP)APIAPIEthernetHardwareSecurityModuleC-V2X ModuleDead ReckoningGN

content.title: KoMoDnext - Automatisiertes Fahren im digitalen Testfeld Düsseldorf : Schlussbericht des Verbundes : Laufzeit des Vorhabens: von: 01.01.2020 bis: 31.03.2022
content.abstract_or_snippet: und B übergreifende Themen unterstütze ZF während der gesamten Projektlaufzeit. Randbedingungen Eine wesentliche Randbedingung beim Datenaustausch mit den Fahrzeugen war und ist das Thema Datenschutz. Hier wurde durch geeignete Voraggregation und Anonymisierung sichergestellt, dass die Vorgaben der EU DSGVO eingehalten werden. Versuchsträger des Instituts für Kraftfahrzeuge der RWTH Aachen Im Rahmen des Projekts wurde in Abstimmung mit den Projektpartnern eine generische und Use Case übergreifende Kommunikationsschnittstelle entwickelt, welche sowohl über ITS-G5 (Nahfeldkommunikation/WLAN 802.11p), als auch Mobilfunk (LTE/4G), parallel Informationen austauschen kann. Hierbei kommen für die beiden Kommunikationswege unterschiedliche Module zum Einsatz. Für die Nahfeldkommunikation wird eine Cohda Wireless MK5 Onbord Unit (OBU) verwendet. Diese ist aus dem Vorgängerprojekt KoMoD verfügbar und wurde um eine PKI- Integration (Public Key Infrastructure) erweitert, um eine Signierung von V2X-Nachrichten zu ermöglichen. Die Mobilfunkkommunikation erfolgt hingegen über einen, von Vodafone bereitgestellten V2X-Connector. Die Software der beiden Module ist dabei so gestaltet, dass die erzeugten Nachrichten im gleichen Format von beiden Modulen verarbeitet und weitergeleitet werden können. Die Module erwarten dabei eine ASN.1-Kodierung der Nachrichten, die von entsprechenden Verarbeitungsalgorithmen vorgenommen werden muss. Neben der Kommunikations-Hardware ist auch die Definition der auszutauschenden Nachrichtentypen eine relevante Grundlage für die Charakterisierung der V2X-Schnittstelle. Dabei wurden die von der ETSI standardisierten Nachrichtentypen verwendet. Relevant sind vor allem die folgenden Nachrichtentypen:  DENM  CAM  MAP  SPAT  IVI  CPM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message) (Cooperative Awareness Message) (MapData Messages) (Signal Phase And Timing Messages) (Infrastructure to Vehicle Information) (Collective Perception Message) Um die,

content.abstract_or_snippet: die Werte für die erlaubte Latenz bei maximal 10ms bis 500ms und für die Reliabilität oberhalb der Werte zwischen 90% und 99.999%. 11 SMART Projekt 2 Grundlagen 2.3.2 Typen und Inhalte von V2X-Nachrichten Der Standard „Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)“ [3] sieht zur Koordination autonom fahrender Fahrzeuge einen periodischen Nachrichtenaustausch zwischen den Fahrzeugen vor. Hierbei wird prinzipiell zwischen zwei Nachrichtentypen unterschieden. Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM) Das sind periodisch gesendete Nachrichten, die Informationen über Position, Bewegung, Fahrzeu- geigenschaften enthalten. [4] Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages (DENM) Diese Nachrichten werden durch (sicherheitskritische) Verkehrssituationen ausgelöste, die bei- spielsweise eine Notbremsung oder einen Überholvorgang einleiten. Die Nachrichten werden, so- lange die Situation besteht, periodisch wiederholt. [5] Zusätzlich sind in [2] weitere Nachrichten definiert, die in ähnlichen Formaten wie CAM und/oder DENM ausgetauscht werden. Hierbei unterscheiden sich Nachrichteninhalt, Paketgrößen und Dienstgüteanforderungen abhängig vom angestrebten Automatisierungsgrad und der V2X-Anwen- dung. Es sind folgende Nachrichteninhalte definiert. Wahrnehmungsdaten Das sind Informationen, die den Wahrnehmungsbereich eines Fahrzeuges erweitern. Mögliche Nachrichteninhalte sind vorverarbeiteten Sensordaten, die abstrakte Informationen über detektierte Objekt enthalten, und hochauflösenden Wahrnehmungsdaten wie Sensorrohdaten einer Kamera oder eines Light Detection and Ranging (LIDAR) Systems. Fahrabsichten Informationen zur Koordination von Fahrmanövern. Es wird zwischen groben Ereignissen wie der Absicht zum Spurwechsel oder Anhalten zu definierbaren Zeitpunkten und Positionen und detail- lierten Trajektorien unterschieden. Kartendaten Nachrichteninhalt sind hochauflösende Kartendaten, die periodisch in

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D1.1: Deliverable D1.1: Use cases, requirements, performance evaluation criteria
content.abstract_or_snippet: relevant VRU 3. Warning Indication 4. Detection that situation is cleared 5. Cancellation of Warning Indication Figure 7.8-2: Message Sequence at HV side 1) The application processor receives CAM messages. 2) The application processor detects that there is a potentially hazardous situation due to a VRU user in the proximity. 3) A warning is indicated to the DVI together with a validity time. After expiry of a given validity time, the warning indication is automatically cancelled at the DVI. 4) It is detected that the hazardous situation has disappeared before the previously indicated expiry time has elapsed 5) A warning cancellation is indicated to the DVI which stops indication of the previously issued warning immediately. Measurements To analyze transmission and processing latencies as well as reliability of message reception, it is sufficient to log all CAM sent and received by the VRU station(s) and the vehicular ITS station(s) involved in the test scenario. It is assumed that all logged messages are time stamped using precise GNSS time information. The Position location accuracy as defined in Section 8.4 is assumed to be sufficient even for VRU use cases. However, it is expected that this requirement in many practical situations cannot be fulfilled due to unfavorable conditions for GNSS signals. Derivation of practical requirements on position location accuracy is one of the objectives for the trials of VRU use cases. Expected Outcome This use case will demonstrate the safety benefit which can be obtained when VRUs, such as e.g. road workers, pedestrians and bicyclists are willing to carry portable devices supporting V2X technology. Compared to other V2X technologies, C-V2X has the inherent advantage that it can be expected to be integrated more easily and more cost efficient into mobile phones targeting at the mass market. This would make it feasible that potentially every individual carrying a mobile phone which supports C- ConVeX Project Confidential 80 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1 V2X can acquire the safety benefits. If the integration of V2P into ordinary phones is not feasible, this use case will be limited only to a special class of traffic participants, such as e.g. road workers. The trial of this use case is also expected to provide very useful findings and awareness, how to manage the communication due to V2P use cases when this service is enabled in areas with very large penetration, e.g. cross-walks in cities

Programs written for this purpose are called Link Level Simulators (LLSs). In this section, we consider the two candidate technologies – ITS-G5 and C-V2X and show their performance in terms of Block Error Rate (BLER) versus SNR. ITS-G5 The 802.11p equivalent in the European C-ITS stack covering PHY and MAC layers is termed as ITS- G5. Similar to its US counterpart, DSRC, it also operates in the 5.9 GHz band. The packet structure of 802.11p is shown in Figure 4-1. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D3.1: Deliverable D3.1: Comparative analysis of candidate V2X radio technologies
content.abstract_or_snippet: for this but relies on the 3GPP interfaces in a hybrid fashion. There are some commonalities but as well as some fundamental differences between the 3GPP PC5 based C-V2X and the ETSI ITS-G5 V2X approaches. The main difference exists in the lower layers of MAC and PHY, and the integration within the whole protocol stack up to the service/application layer mainly with respect to management and security. Section 5 is providing these insights and comparison in more detail. ConVeX Project 16 (77) Deliverable D3.1: Comparative Analysis of Candidate V2X Radio Technologies V1.0 Analysis of V2X Technologies Introduction The present section 5 describes the main functions and characteristics of the current candidate V2X radio technologies: • DSRC/WAVE specified by the US Department of Transportation (U.S. DOT) • ETSI C-ITS / ITS-G5 specified by ETSI by mandate of the European Commission • 3GPP Rel-14 Cellular V2X based on LTE Uu and PC5 radio technologies Both DSRC/WAVE and ETSI C-ITS / ITS-G5 are based on IEEE 802.11 WLAN radio technologies, namely a MAC/PHY variant of the 802.11a standard which is denoted IEEE 802.11p. Therefore, both technologies are colloquially often referred to as “WLAN-based” or “WiFi-based” V2X technologies. Section 5.2 introduces the overall protocol stacks of DSRC/WAVE and ETSI C- ITS / ITS-G5 and elaborates the commonalities and differences between these technologies. Section 5.3 presents the main features of 3GPP Rel-14 LTE-based cellular V2X radio technologies examined in the ConVeX project, namely LTE Uu radio technology applied for V2N communications, and LTE PC5 (“sidelink”) radio technology for V2V/V2I/V2P communications. Section 5.4 provides an analysis of the main performance characteristics of the considered V2X technologies. 802.11p-based Technologies Introduction to 802.11p, DSRC and ITS-G5 The IEEE 802.11p specification has been developed as an amendment [31] to the IEEE 802.11 WLAN standard series of specifications. The goal of this effort was to introduce radio technology enhancements in order to support communication between vehicles (V2V) and between vehicle and road side infrastructure

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D3.1: Deliverable D3.1: Comparative analysis of candidate V2X radio technologies
content.abstract_or_snippet: ITS-G5 Tx of IPv6 packets using geographical routing EN302636-5-1 Transport layer EN302637-2 EN302637-3 CAM DENM TS36 series Uu interface As can be identified from Table 4.1-2 the 3GPP C-V2X focus is on providing the lower layer (MAC and PHY) functionality to be integrated with the ITS protocol stack and services specified by ETSI. More details on the C-V2X technology and protocol stacks (PC5, Uu) can be found in the other relevant project descriptions, e.g., [30], and in Section 5 of this document. ConVeX Project 15 (77) Deliverable D3.1: Comparative Analysis of Candidate V2X Radio Technologies V1.0 Architectures and Reference Points This section provides a quick review, overview and comparison of V2X technologies with reference to further sources of information. Figure 4.2-1 shows the high-level architecture with the relevant use case categories and the relevant interfaces provided by the technologies of ETSI and 3GPP, respectively. Figure 4.2-1 High Level Network Architecture and Technology/Protocol Support The V2X specifications in 3GPP Release 14 and in ETSI, respectively, include up to two complementary communication interfaces. Direct communication such as vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) is realized through PC5 (ProSe Communication reference point 5). The same holds for vehicle-to- pedestrian (V2P) and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communications. The respective ETSI protocol for these communications is ITS-G5 based on IEEE 802.11p as outlined in the previous section already. In addition, the LTE - or in future the 5G NR - Uu connection complements any communication for the non-direct links within the (wide area) network for V2N cases. This connection might also be used as backhaul-link between road-side-units (RSU) and their (traffic management) center, acting as transparent IP connection. This interface is solely supported by the 3GPP LTE (4G) and 5G-NR Uu interfaces; ETSI does not provide an own interface for this but relies on the 3GPP interfaces in a hybrid fashion. There are some commonalities but as well as some fundamental differences between the 3GPP PC5 based C-V2X and the ETSI ITS-G5

content.title: Schlussbericht GAIA-X 4 AMS - Advanced mobility services
content.abstract_or_snippet: – von Pilotprojekten zu ersten realen Einsätzen. 5G Roll-Out ermöglicht niedrigere Latenzen, höhere Zuverlässigkeit und Netzwerk-Slicing für 5G-C-V2X. ITS-G5 bleibt relevant für lokale Szenarien, zunehmend werden hybride Multi-RAT-Ansätze (ITS-G5 + C-V2X + UWB) erforscht. Fahrzeug-Sensorik (Kamera, Radar, LiDAR) wird erstmals mit V2X-Daten fusioniert, wodurch kooperative Wahrnehmung und Kollisionsvermeidung verbessert werden. Day-2 und Day-3 Anwendungsfälle rücken in den Fokus. Kooperative ACC oder V2P-Kommunikation mit Fußgängern erweitern den Fokus über Warnfunktionen hinaus. 3GPP Releases 16-18 und ETSI-Updates standardisieren Kommunikation, Sicherheit (PKI) und Interoperabilität. Seite 29 von 30 Europa Mobilitätsdatenräumen die Integration in Daten- und Identitätsökosysteme voran. treibt mit C-Roads, Car2Car Communications Consortium und Gaia-X Parallel entstehen Edge-basierte Architekturen, die lokale Rechenleistung und KI in V2X einbinden. Steigende Anzahl an Serienfahrzeuge mit V2X-Modulen und kommende Infrastruktur- Rollouts (z.B. ITS Cube Hamburg) markieren den Übergang zur Anwendung. Offene Herausforderungen bleiben Kosten und Durchdringung. In Summe: V2X entwickelte sich von isolierter Fahrzeugkommunikation zu einem integrierten, daten- und sensorbasierten Kooperationssystem für vernetzte schnellen) Mobilität mit wachsender Marktpenetration. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D2.2: Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX system architecture description
content.abstract_or_snippet: TS 102 731 defines the high-level security architecture based on a Threat, Vulnerability, Risk Analysis (TVRA) using the results of [17] • ETSI TS 102 940 defines security requirements and the overall security architecture of the ETSI ITS system. • ETSI TS 102 941 defines the messages exchanged between ITS stations with Enrolment Authorities (EA) and Authorization Authorities (AA) • ETSI TS 102 942 specifies details of Authentication and Authorization Services for CAM and DENM • ETSI TS 102 943 specifies services recommended for confidentiality protection • ETSI TS 103 097 defines the frame format of secured C-ITS messages including the binary format of applicable certificate representations. Figure 6.5-1: Specifications defining the ETSI ITS Security Framework ETSI ITS Security Architecture The ITS security architecture is comprised of the security mechanisms and management functions applied to the messages (e.g. CAM, DENM) exchanged between vehicular ITS stations, roadside ITS stations and the network via the V2X interfaces (PC5 and Uu) which are based on public key certificates, and a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). The PKI is required to manage the security credentials employed for securing V2X communication. A public key certificate includes the public key, information about the identity of the owner of the public key (called the subject), and the digital signature of an entity (denoted Certification ConVeX Project 58 (76) TR 102 893Threat AnalysisTS 102 893High-Level Security ArchitectureTS 102 940Security Architecture & ManagementTS 102 941Trust & Privacy TS 102 942Access ControlTS 102 943ConfidentialityTS 103 097Security Header & Certificate Formats Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX System Architecture Description Version: V1.0 Authority) that has verified the certificate's contents (called the issuer of the certificate). If the signature is valid, and the software examining the certificate trusts the issuer, then it can use that public key to communicate securely with the certificate's subject. As part of a certificate enrolment procedure, a secret private key is generated together with the certificate which includes the public key. Figure 6.5-2 shows the PKI of the ETSI ITS security architecture. The PKI is comprised of the following entities: • Enrolment Authority (EA) In generic PKI terminology, an EA represents a Registration Authority (RA).

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D2.2: Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX system architecture description
content.abstract_or_snippet: the PC5 LTE sidelink interface, the PDCP layer provides functions for integrity and confidentiality protection [30], [31]. This includes functionality for key management Use of Transport Layer Security (TLS or DTLS) is currently not addressed in [12] and [24]. However, TLS/DTLS might be a reasonable choice for end-to-end security protection of communication when using the V2N/Uu interfaces, including V2N2V communication. Security Mechanisms Supported by the C-V2X Communication Platform This section briefly describes the security mechanisms planned to be supported by the C-V2X communication platform. CAM and DENM will comply with the ETSI ITS security header as defined in ETSI TS 103 097 [18] and described in Section 6.5.3.4 above. All C-V2X communication modules employed in the ConVeX test system will use pre- provisioned certificates. These certificates will be installed as part of the Application and ITS stack software installation procedure. At the present point in time, it is not decided whether or not functionality for remote re- enrolment/re-assignment of certificates will be provided. If such functionality is supported, it will likely not fully comply with ETSI PKI architecture as outlined in section 6.5.2 and instead be implemented in a more simple way where essentially all entities are combined on a single physical cloud server. Authentication and authorization of DENM and CAM will be performed based on the end-user identities associated with the deployed certificates. Integrity protection of messages will be applied using a suitable signature algorithm of the Digital Signature Standard recommended by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), e.g., ECDSA with nistp256 curve and SHA256 as recommended in ETSI TS 103 097 [19]. The exact details of the signature algorithms employed by the test system are to be defined. V2V and V2I (i.e., LTE sidelink/PC5) one-to-one unicast services supported by the ConVeX test system can be secured by using the security mechanisms specified in clause 6.5 of 3GPP 33.303 [31]. For unicast services requiring TCP/IP or UDP/IP a suitable choice for end-to-end security protection between ITS stations and the cloud and between ITS stations is to use certificate-based Transport Layer Security, using TLS 1.2 [28] or DTLS 1.2 [29]. content.abstract_or_snippet: the PC5 LTE sidelink interface, the PDCP layer provides functions for integrity and confidentiality protection [30], [31]. This includes functionality for key management Use of Transport Layer Security (TLS or DTLS) is currently not addressed in [12] and [24]. However, TLS/DTLS might be a reasonable choice for end-to-end security protection of communication when using the V2N/Uu interfaces, including V2N2V communication. Security Mechanisms Supported by the C-V2X Communication Platform This section briefly describes the security mechanisms planned to be supported by the C-V2X communication platform. CAM and DENM will comply with the ETSI ITS security header as defined in ETSI TS 103 097 [18] and described in Section 6.5.3.4 above. All C-V2X communication modules employed in the ConVeX test system will use pre- provisioned certificates. These certificates will be installed as part of the Application and ITS stack software installation procedure. At the present point in time, it is not decided whether or not functionality for remote re- enrolment/re-assignment of certificates will be provided. If such functionality is supported, it will likely not fully comply with ETSI PKI architecture as outlined in section 6.5.2 and instead be implemented in a more simple way where essentially all entities are combined on a single physical cloud server. Authentication and authorization of DENM and CAM will be performed based on the end-user identities associated with the deployed certificates. Integrity protection of messages will be applied using a suitable signature algorithm of the Digital Signature Standard recommended by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), e.g., ECDSA with nistp256 curve and SHA256 as recommended in ETSI TS 103 097 [19]. The exact details of the signature algorithms employed by the test system are to be defined. V2V and V2I (i.e., LTE sidelink/PC5) one-to-one unicast services supported by the ConVeX test system can be secured by using the security mechanisms specified in clause 6.5 of 3GPP 33.303 [31]. For unicast services requiring TCP/IP or UDP/IP a suitable choice for end-to-end security protection between ITS stations and the cloud and between ITS stations is to use certificate-based Transport Layer Security, using TLS 1.2 [28] or DTLS 1.2 [29]. content.title: KoMoD Schlussbericht : digitales Testfeld  Düsseldorf : 01.06.2017-31.08.2019
content.abstract_or_snippet: Förderkennzeichen 16AVF1006A 11. Seitenzahl 66 13. Literaturangaben - 14. Tabellen 8 15. Abbildungen 41 18. Kurzfassung Die Entwicklungen von Anwendungen des vernetzten und automatisierten Fahrens schreiten zügig voran. Viele Applikationen werden aber zunächst auf abgeschlossen Testfeldern erprobt. Die nächsten Entwicklungsschritte umfassend Testprogramme der verfügbaren Technologien in realen Verkehrssituationen. Zudem ist zu klären, inwieweit die bestehende Infrastruktur die Anwendungen unterstützen kann. Ziel des Forschungsvorhabens war die Bereitstellung eines Testfeldes zur Erprobung neuer Technologien der Fahrzeug- Infrastrukturvernetzung sowie des teil- und hochautomatisierten Fahrens unter realen Verkehrsbedingungen. Adressiert wurden insbesondere folgende Aufgabenbereiche:     Erprobung der Datenbereitstellung und Kommunikation. Analyse des Zusammenspiels von Informationsbereitstellung durch die Infrastruktur und Erfassung durch die bordautonome Sensorik im Fahrzeug. Analyse des Zusammenspiels der fahrzeugseitigen Systeme mit hochpräzisem Kartenmaterial unter Nutzung geeigneter Ortungstechnologien. Bewertung der umgesetzten Anwendungen zur Unterstützung von automatisierten Fahrfunktionen hinsichtlich der Auswirkungen auf Verkehrseffizienz, Verkehrssicherheit sowie auf Umwelteinträge. Zusammen mit Partnern aus der Verkehrstechnik und der Automobilzuliefererindustrie wurden im Testfeld V2X-Services bereitgestellt, die Fahrzeuge über Daten und Informationen mit der Infrastruktur vernetzen. Auf dieser Grundlage wurden umfangreiche Felderprobungen mit verschiedenen Versuchsträgerfahrzeugen durchgeführt. Diese wurden im Testfeld Düsseldorf auch verkehrsträgerübergreifend (MIV, ÖPNV und Radverkehr) aufgesetzt. Im Testbetrieb konnten wertvol

content.title: ACCorD - Korridor für neue Mobilität Aachen-Düsseldorf : Schlussbericht des Verbundes : Laufzeit des Vorhabens: von: 01.01.2020 (M1) bis: 31.03.2022 (M27)
content.abstract_or_snippet: modellen von Verkehrsteilnehmenden. Abschließend bietet die simulative Absicherung ein großes Potenzial, um neue Fahrfunktionen szenarienbasiert in der Simulation zu testen. Online können hingegen V2X-Nachrichten (CPMs) an automatisierte und vernetzte Forschungsfahrzeuge versendet werden, um die aktive Fahrfunktion durch externe Infrastrukturinformationen zu ergänzen und einen sicheren Fahrbetrieb zu gewährleisten. Die Verbreitung der V2X-Nachrichten kann dabei zentral über Mobilfunk (C-V2X) oder lokal über ITS-G5 RSU an den einzelnen Messstationen erfolgen. Die im Projekt errichtete Testumgebung soll auch nach Projektende für vollständig, das heißt nicht nur einzelne Testabschnitte, weiter für Forschungszwecke genutzt werden und der interessierten Öffentlichkeit zur Verfügung gestellt werden. Hier soll neben der Nutzung in ACCorD_Schlussbericht_v3.0, 31.05.2022 (M29) Datenfusion & TrackingTrajektorienSzenarienLive-Visualisierung & V2X⁞SzenarienextraktionDatenzugriffZentralerServer zurDatenverarbeitungund Administration FKZ 01MM19001A-G 42 öffentlich geförderten Forschungsprojekten auch die Möglichkeit einer Nutzung im Rahmen von Industrieforschung geprüft werden. Auf diese Weise könnte der Wirtschaftsstanddort Deutschland von der errichteten Testumgebung und dem entwickelten Know-how direkt profitieren, zielführend könnte dabei auch eine forcierte Nutzung durch KMUs sein. Wie im Projektantrag formuliert, könnte auf dies Weise auch die zum Weiterbetrieb erforderlichen Mittel erwirtschaftet werden. Außerdem wird so ein breiter Zugang für interessierte externe Nutzende geschaffen. Dabei ist sicherzustellen, dass die Nutzung für wissenschaftlichen Industrieforschung nachranging gegenüber der wissenschaftlichen hoheitlichen Nutzung erfolgen soll. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D1.1: Deliverable D1.1: Use cases, requirements, performance evaluation criteria
content.abstract_or_snippet: attributes as part of the V2V Service). The V2V payload must be flexible in order to accommodate different information contents. V2V is predominantly broadcast-based. Where a direct communication might not be possible e.g. due to the limited direct communication range, the V2V related application information exchange might be done using other V2X services alternatively, e.g. using additional infrastructure like RSU or application servers and the different underlying communication paths of V2I, V2N etc. potentially in a combined manner. Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) The V2I communication will also be implemented using LTE Sidelink Transmission Mode 4 on PC5, i.e. the RSU acts similar as a UE. Note that the 3GPP standard also foresees a variant of a V2I communication via Uu link, then assuming the RSU to be part of an eNode B, but this setup is again not used within this trial as it is no probable option for a future real world implementation. The UE supporting V2I applications transmits messages containing V2I application information to an RSU. The RSU and/or the locally relevant application server transmit messages containing V2I application information to one or more UEs supporting V2I applications. A locally relevant application server serves a particular geographic area. There can be multiple application servers serving overlapping areas, providing the same or different applications. Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P) V2P communication is very similar to V2V, also using PC5 LTE Sidelink Transmission Mode 4. The UE supporting V2P applications transmits application layer information. Such information can be broadcast by a vehicle with UE supporting V2X Service (e.g., warning to pedestrian), and/or by a pedestrian with UE supporting V2X Service (e.g., warning to vehicle). ConVeX Project Confidential 14 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1 Vehicle to Network (V2N) V2N is also introduced where one party is a UE and the other party is a serving entity, supporting both V2N applications and communicating with each other via cellular network (e.g. LTE or 5G). This means for the LTE case, the Uu interface will be used. Network to Vehicle/UE communication can be multicast or unicast. The serving entities are usually connected to public internet, and possibly to the backends of several

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D1.1: Deliverable D1.1: Use cases, requirements, performance evaluation criteria
content.abstract_or_snippet: 1 ConVeX High Level Network Architecture Communication connections in the ConVeX system architecture can be classified in the following way: Direct communication such as vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) is realized through PC5 (ProSe Communication reference point 5). The same holds true for vehicle-to-pedestrians (V2P) and vehicle- to-infrastructure (V2I). In addition the LTE-Uu connection complements any communication in the non-direct links within the (wide area) network. This connection will also be used as backhaul-link between road-side-units (RSU) and its (traffic management) center, acting as transparent IP connection. Figure 4.2-1 ConVeX High Level Network Architecture Types of V2X The vehicular communication referred to as Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X), contains following four different types:  Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communications ConVeX Project Confidential 13 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1  Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) Communications  Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P) Communications  Vehicle-to-Network (V2N) Figure 4.2-1. illustrates these types of communications. Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) V2V communication is realized with the means of LTE V2V Sidelink on PC5 interface enabling a direct vehicle to vehicle communication. The LTE standard allows two different transmission modes on PC5: the so-called “in-coverage” mode, where an eNode B is scheduling the actual transmissions of the UE (Sidelink Transmission Mode 3), as well as the “out-of-coverage” mode where there is no eNode B scheduling involved, but an autonomous resource selection performed by the UEs (Sidelink Transmission Mode 4). The latter will be used for the trial and is also envisioned as the most relevant mode of operation for future real world usage. As mentioned above, the same PC5 communication will be used for V2I and V2P. The UE supporting V2V applications transmits application layer information (e.g. about its location, dynamics, and attributes as part of the V2V Service). The V2V payload must be flexible in order to accommodate different information contents. V2V is predominantly broadcast-based. Where a direct communication might not be possible e.g. due to the limited direct

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D4.1: Deliverable D4.1: Roadside ITS station specification
content.abstract_or_snippet: Service: A type of V2X Service, where one party is a UE and the other party is an RSU both using V2I application. V2N Service: A type of V2X Service, where one party is a UE and the other party is a serving entity, both using V2N applications and communicating with each other via cellular network (e.g. LTE or 5G) . V2P Service: A type of V2X Service, where both parties of the communication are UEs using V2P application. For this service the vehicle-side UE is a V-ITS-S, the pedestrian-side (respectively VRU-side) UE is a P-ITS-S. V2V Service: A type of V2X Service, where both parties of the communication are UEs using V2V application. For this service both vehicle-side UEs represent V-ITS-S. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D2.2: Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX system architecture description
content.abstract_or_snippet: this trial as it is no probable option for a future real world implementation. ConVeX Project 12 (76) Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX System Architecture Description Version: V1.0 Description of System Components C-V2X Communication Platform Overview The C-V2X Communication Platform can be used for car and roadside unit integration and supports advanced use cases for V2V, V2I, V2P and V2N. It is supposed to be used for development and testing of trial specific use cases. The main hardware component for the trial system is the C-V2X Communication Platform. Figure 5.1-1 shows a simplified structural and functional overview of the prototype implementation with its corresponding components. It is composed of the C-V2X Development Platform (C-V2X DP) shown on the right side linked via an Ethernet connection to the Communication Module (CCARD - Connected Car Application Reference Design) shown on the left side. In the trial setup, the CCARD is used for the V2N communication via an LTE network. The C-V2X Development Platform consists of the Application Processor (AP) module and the C- V2X Module and peripheral components. These two modules are linked and communicate via an Ethernet connection. The C-V2X DP furthermore provides external connectivity via Ethernet, CAN and USB. Figure 5.1-1: C-V2X Communication Platform (simplified) Architecture The AP module of the C-V2X Development Platform consists of a Qualcomm Snapdragon APQ8096 which runs the software providing the lower layer services, the ITS Stack and the individual V2X applications. Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) are provided to enable the usage of the different services. The figure shows the two most important APIs giving access to the ITS Facility layer as well as the lower entry point to e.g. use the modem functionalities. In addition, the AP also runs the security management functions which will ensure proper handling of the security credentials. The APQ8096 verifies ITS message security, evaluates position ConVeX Project 13 (76) 5.9 GHz(V2V/V2I/V2P)C-V2X Communication PlatformGNSSLTECommunication Module(CCARD)(LTE Network Connectivity Only)700/2600 MHz(V2N)Data ServiceEthernetEthernetCANUSBV2X ApplicationsFacility serviceSecurity serviceModem/GNSS/CAN servicesApplication Processor (AP)APIAPIEthernetHardwareSecurityModuleC-V2X ModuleDead ReckoningGN

content.title: KoMoDnext - Automatisiertes Fahren im digitalen Testfeld Düsseldorf : Schlussbericht des Verbundes : Laufzeit des Vorhabens: von: 01.01.2020 bis: 31.03.2022
content.abstract_or_snippet: und B übergreifende Themen unterstütze ZF während der gesamten Projektlaufzeit. Randbedingungen Eine wesentliche Randbedingung beim Datenaustausch mit den Fahrzeugen war und ist das Thema Datenschutz. Hier wurde durch geeignete Voraggregation und Anonymisierung sichergestellt, dass die Vorgaben der EU DSGVO eingehalten werden. Versuchsträger des Instituts für Kraftfahrzeuge der RWTH Aachen Im Rahmen des Projekts wurde in Abstimmung mit den Projektpartnern eine generische und Use Case übergreifende Kommunikationsschnittstelle entwickelt, welche sowohl über ITS-G5 (Nahfeldkommunikation/WLAN 802.11p), als auch Mobilfunk (LTE/4G), parallel Informationen austauschen kann. Hierbei kommen für die beiden Kommunikationswege unterschiedliche Module zum Einsatz. Für die Nahfeldkommunikation wird eine Cohda Wireless MK5 Onbord Unit (OBU) verwendet. Diese ist aus dem Vorgängerprojekt KoMoD verfügbar und wurde um eine PKI- Integration (Public Key Infrastructure) erweitert, um eine Signierung von V2X-Nachrichten zu ermöglichen. Die Mobilfunkkommunikation erfolgt hingegen über einen, von Vodafone bereitgestellten V2X-Connector. Die Software der beiden Module ist dabei so gestaltet, dass die erzeugten Nachrichten im gleichen Format von beiden Modulen verarbeitet und weitergeleitet werden können. Die Module erwarten dabei eine ASN.1-Kodierung der Nachrichten, die von entsprechenden Verarbeitungsalgorithmen vorgenommen werden muss. Neben der Kommunikations-Hardware ist auch die Definition der auszutauschenden Nachrichtentypen eine relevante Grundlage für die Charakterisierung der V2X-Schnittstelle. Dabei wurden die von der ETSI standardisierten Nachrichtentypen verwendet. Relevant sind vor allem die folgenden Nachrichtentypen:  DENM  CAM  MAP  SPAT  IVI  CPM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message) (Cooperative Awareness Message) (MapData Messages) (Signal Phase And Timing Messages) (Infrastructure to Vehicle Information) (Collective Perception Message) Um die,

content.title: SMART - Simulation von Mobilfunk und Automobilverhalten in Realzeit : Abschlussbericht : KMU-Innovationsoffensive IKT, Forschungsbereich Kommunikationssysteme; IT-Sicherheit : Projektlaufzeit: 01.09.2017-30.01.2021
content.abstract_or_snippet: die Werte für die erlaubte Latenz bei maximal 10ms bis 500ms und für die Reliabilität oberhalb der Werte zwischen 90% und 99.999%. 11 SMART Projekt 2 Grundlagen 2.3.2 Typen und Inhalte von V2X-Nachrichten Der Standard „Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)“ [3] sieht zur Koordination autonom fahrender Fahrzeuge einen periodischen Nachrichtenaustausch zwischen den Fahrzeugen vor. Hierbei wird prinzipiell zwischen zwei Nachrichtentypen unterschieden. Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM) Das sind periodisch gesendete Nachrichten, die Informationen über Position, Bewegung, Fahrzeu- geigenschaften enthalten. [4] Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages (DENM) Diese Nachrichten werden durch (sicherheitskritische) Verkehrssituationen ausgelöste, die bei- spielsweise eine Notbremsung oder einen Überholvorgang einleiten. Die Nachrichten werden, so- lange die Situation besteht, periodisch wiederholt. [5] Zusätzlich sind in [2] weitere Nachrichten definiert, die in ähnlichen Formaten wie CAM und/oder DENM ausgetauscht werden. Hierbei unterscheiden sich Nachrichteninhalt, Paketgrößen und Dienstgüteanforderungen abhängig vom angestrebten Automatisierungsgrad und der V2X-Anwen- dung. Es sind folgende Nachrichteninhalte definiert. Wahrnehmungsdaten Das sind Informationen, die den Wahrnehmungsbereich eines Fahrzeuges erweitern. Mögliche Nachrichteninhalte sind vorverarbeiteten Sensordaten, die abstrakte Informationen über detektierte Objekt enthalten, und hochauflösenden Wahrnehmungsdaten wie Sensorrohdaten einer Kamera oder eines Light Detection and Ranging (LIDAR) Systems. Fahrabsichten Informationen zur Koordination von Fahrmanövern. Es wird zwischen groben Ereignissen wie der Absicht zum Spurwechsel oder Anhalten zu definierbaren Zeitpunkten und Positionen und detail- lierten Trajektorien unterschieden. Kartendaten Nachrichteninhalt sind hochauflösende Kartendaten, die periodisch in

content.abstract_or_snippet: Inhalte von V2X-Nachrichten Der Standard „Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)“ [3] sieht zur Koordination autonom fahrender Fahrzeuge einen periodischen Nachrichtenaustausch zwischen den Fahrzeugen vor. Hierbei wird prinzipiell zwischen zwei Nachrichtentypen unterschieden. Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM) Das sind periodisch gesendete Nachrichten, die Informationen über Position, Bewegung, Fahrzeu- geigenschaften enthalten. [4] Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages (DENM) Diese Nachrichten werden durch (sicherheitskritische) Verkehrssituationen ausgelöste, die bei- spielsweise eine Notbremsung oder einen Überholvorgang einleiten. Die Nachrichten werden, so- lange die Situation besteht, periodisch wiederholt. [5] Zusätzlich sind in [2] weitere Nachrichten definiert, die in ähnlichen Formaten wie CAM und/oder DENM ausgetauscht werden. Hierbei unterscheiden sich Nachrichteninhalt, Paketgrößen und Dienstgüteanforderungen abhängig vom angestrebten Automatisierungsgrad und der V2X-Anwen- dung. Es sind folgende Nachrichteninhalte definiert. Wahrnehmungsdaten Das sind Informationen, die den Wahrnehmungsbereich eines Fahrzeuges erweitern. Mögliche Nachrichteninhalte sind vorverarbeiteten Sensordaten, die abstrakte Informationen über detektierte Objekt enthalten, und hochauflösenden Wahrnehmungsdaten wie Sensorrohdaten einer Kamera oder eines Light Detection and Ranging (LIDAR) Systems. Fahrabsichten Informationen zur Koordination von Fahrmanövern. Es wird zwischen groben Ereignissen wie der Absicht zum Spurwechsel oder Anhalten zu definierbaren Zeitpunkten und Positionen und detail- lierten Trajektorien unterschieden. Kartendaten Nachrichteninhalt sind hochauflösende Kartendaten, die periodisch in die Fahrzeuge heruntergela- den werden. Die Kartendaten können die Positionen und Bewegungen nicht kommunikationsfähi- ger Verkehrsteilnehmer enthalten. Die technische Voraussetzung für autonomes Fahren mit V2X-

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D1.1: Deliverable D1.1: Use cases, requirements, performance evaluation criteria
content.abstract_or_snippet: relevant VRU 3. Warning Indication 4. Detection that situation is cleared 5. Cancellation of Warning Indication Figure 7.8-2: Message Sequence at HV side 1) The application processor receives CAM messages. 2) The application processor detects that there is a potentially hazardous situation due to a VRU user in the proximity. 3) A warning is indicated to the DVI together with a validity time. After expiry of a given validity time, the warning indication is automatically cancelled at the DVI. 4) It is detected that the hazardous situation has disappeared before the previously indicated expiry time has elapsed 5) A warning cancellation is indicated to the DVI which stops indication of the previously issued warning immediately. Measurements To analyze transmission and processing latencies as well as reliability of message reception, it is sufficient to log all CAM sent and received by the VRU station(s) and the vehicular ITS station(s) involved in the test scenario. It is assumed that all logged messages are time stamped using precise GNSS time information. The Position location accuracy as defined in Section 8.4 is assumed to be sufficient even for VRU use cases. However, it is expected that this requirement in many practical situations cannot be fulfilled due to unfavorable conditions for GNSS signals. Derivation of practical requirements on position location accuracy is one of the objectives for the trials of VRU use cases. Expected Outcome This use case will demonstrate the safety benefit which can be obtained when VRUs, such as e.g. road workers, pedestrians and bicyclists are willing to carry portable devices supporting V2X technology. Compared to other V2X technologies, C-V2X has the inherent advantage that it can be expected to be integrated more easily and more cost efficient into mobile phones targeting at the mass market. This would make it feasible that potentially every individual carrying a mobile phone which supports C- ConVeX Project Confidential 80 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1 V2X can acquire the safety benefits. If the integration of V2P into ordinary phones is not feasible, this use case will be limited only to a special class of traffic participants, such as e.g. road workers. The trial of this use case is also expected to provide very useful findings and awareness, how to manage the communication due to V2P use cases when this service is enabled in areas with very large penetration, e.g. cross-walks in cities

Programs written for this purpose are called Link Level Simulators (LLSs). In this section, we consider the two candidate technologies – ITS-G5 and C-V2X and show their performance in terms of Block Error Rate (BLER) versus SNR. ITS-G5 The 802.11p equivalent in the European C-ITS stack covering PHY and MAC layers is termed as ITS- G5. Similar to its US counterpart, DSRC, it also operates in the 5.9 GHz band. The packet structure of 802.11p is shown in Figure 4-1. Legacy Short Training Field (L-STF) that is used for packet detection, coarse frequency correction, and automatic gain control 2. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D3.1: Deliverable D3.1: Comparative analysis of candidate V2X radio technologies
content.abstract_or_snippet: for this but relies on the 3GPP interfaces in a hybrid fashion. There are some commonalities but as well as some fundamental differences between the 3GPP PC5 based C-V2X and the ETSI ITS-G5 V2X approaches. The main difference exists in the lower layers of MAC and PHY, and the integration within the whole protocol stack up to the service/application layer mainly with respect to management and security. Section 5 is providing these insights and comparison in more detail. ConVeX Project 16 (77) Deliverable D3.1: Comparative Analysis of Candidate V2X Radio Technologies V1.0 Analysis of V2X Technologies Introduction The present section 5 describes the main functions and characteristics of the current candidate V2X radio technologies: • DSRC/WAVE specified by the US Department of Transportation (U.S. DOT) • ETSI C-ITS / ITS-G5 specified by ETSI by mandate of the European Commission • 3GPP Rel-14 Cellular V2X based on LTE Uu and PC5 radio technologies Both DSRC/WAVE and ETSI C-ITS / ITS-G5 are based on IEEE 802.11 WLAN radio technologies, namely a MAC/PHY variant of the 802.11a standard which is denoted IEEE 802.11p. Therefore, both technologies are colloquially often referred to as “WLAN-based” or “WiFi-based” V2X technologies. Section 5.2 introduces the overall protocol stacks of DSRC/WAVE and ETSI C- ITS / ITS-G5 and elaborates the commonalities and differences between these technologies. Section 5.3 presents the main features of 3GPP Rel-14 LTE-based cellular V2X radio technologies examined in the ConVeX project, namely LTE Uu radio technology applied for V2N communications, and LTE PC5 (“sidelink”) radio technology for V2V/V2I/V2P communications. Section 5.4 provides an analysis of the main performance characteristics of the considered V2X technologies. 802.11p-based Technologies Introduction to 802.11p, DSRC and ITS-G5 The IEEE 802.11p specification has been developed as an amendment [31] to the IEEE 802.11 WLAN standard series of specifications. The goal of this effort was to introduce radio technology enhancements in order to support communication between vehicles (V2V) and between vehicle and road side infrastructure

content.abstract_or_snippet: ITS-G5 Tx of IPv6 packets using geographical routing EN302636-5-1 Transport layer EN302637-2 EN302637-3 CAM DENM TS36 series Uu interface As can be identified from Table 4.1-2 the 3GPP C-V2X focus is on providing the lower layer (MAC and PHY) functionality to be integrated with the ITS protocol stack and services specified by ETSI. More details on the C-V2X technology and protocol stacks (PC5, Uu) can be found in the other relevant project descriptions, e.g., [30], and in Section 5 of this document. ConVeX Project 15 (77) Deliverable D3.1: Comparative Analysis of Candidate V2X Radio Technologies V1.0 Architectures and Reference Points This section provides a quick review, overview and comparison of V2X technologies with reference to further sources of information. Figure 4.2-1 shows the high-level architecture with the relevant use case categories and the relevant interfaces provided by the technologies of ETSI and 3GPP, respectively. Figure 4.2-1 High Level Network Architecture and Technology/Protocol Support The V2X specifications in 3GPP Release 14 and in ETSI, respectively, include up to two complementary communication interfaces. Direct communication such as vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) is realized through PC5 (ProSe Communication reference point 5). The same holds for vehicle-to- pedestrian (V2P) and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communications. The respective ETSI protocol for these communications is ITS-G5 based on IEEE 802.11p as outlined in the previous section already. In addition, the LTE - or in future the 5G NR - Uu connection complements any communication for the non-direct links within the (wide area) network for V2N cases. This connection might also be used as backhaul-link between road-side-units (RSU) and their (traffic management) center, acting as transparent IP connection. This interface is solely supported by the 3GPP LTE (4G) and 5G-NR Uu interfaces; ETSI does not provide an own interface for this but relies on the 3GPP interfaces in a hybrid fashion. There are some commonalities but as well as some fundamental differences between the 3GPP PC5 based C-V2X and the ETSI ITS-G5

content.title: Schlussbericht GAIA-X 4 AMS - Advanced mobility services
content.abstract_or_snippet: – von Pilotprojekten zu ersten realen Einsätzen. 5G Roll-Out ermöglicht niedrigere Latenzen, höhere Zuverlässigkeit und Netzwerk-Slicing für 5G-C-V2X. ITS-G5 bleibt relevant für lokale Szenarien, zunehmend werden hybride Multi-RAT-Ansätze (ITS-G5 + C-V2X + UWB) erforscht. Fahrzeug-Sensorik (Kamera, Radar, LiDAR) wird erstmals mit V2X-Daten fusioniert, wodurch kooperative Wahrnehmung und Kollisionsvermeidung verbessert werden. Day-2 und Day-3 Anwendungsfälle rücken in den Fokus. Kooperative ACC oder V2P-Kommunikation mit Fußgängern erweitern den Fokus über Warnfunktionen hinaus. 3GPP Releases 16-18 und ETSI-Updates standardisieren Kommunikation, Sicherheit (PKI) und Interoperabilität. Seite 29 von 30 Europa Mobilitätsdatenräumen die Integration in Daten- und Identitätsökosysteme voran. treibt mit C-Roads, Car2Car Communications Consortium und Gaia-X Parallel entstehen Edge-basierte Architekturen, die lokale Rechenleistung und KI in V2X einbinden. Steigende Anzahl an Serienfahrzeuge mit V2X-Modulen und kommende Infrastruktur- Rollouts (z.B. ITS Cube Hamburg) markieren den Übergang zur Anwendung. Offene Herausforderungen bleiben Kosten und Durchdringung. In Summe: V2X entwickelte sich von isolierter Fahrzeugkommunikation zu einem integrierten, daten- und sensorbasierten Kooperationssystem für vernetzte schnellen) Mobilität mit wachsender Marktpenetration. content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D2.2: Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX system architecture description
content.abstract_or_snippet: TS 102 731 defines the high-level security architecture based on a Threat, Vulnerability, Risk Analysis (TVRA) using the results of [17] • ETSI TS 102 940 defines security requirements and the overall security architecture of the ETSI ITS system. • ETSI TS 102 941 defines the messages exchanged between ITS stations with Enrolment Authorities (EA) and Authorization Authorities (AA) • ETSI TS 102 942 specifies details of Authentication and Authorization Services for CAM and DENM • ETSI TS 102 943 specifies services recommended for confidentiality protection • ETSI TS 103 097 defines the frame format of secured C-ITS messages including the binary format of applicable certificate representations. Figure 6.5-1: Specifications defining the ETSI ITS Security Framework ETSI ITS Security Architecture The ITS security architecture is comprised of the security mechanisms and management functions applied to the messages (e.g. CAM, DENM) exchanged between vehicular ITS stations, roadside ITS stations and the network via the V2X interfaces (PC5 and Uu) which are based on public key certificates, and a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). The PKI is required to manage the security credentials employed for securing V2X communication. A public key certificate includes the public key, information about the identity of the owner of the public key (called the subject), and the digital signature of an entity (denoted Certification ConVeX Project 58 (76) TR 102 893Threat AnalysisTS 102 893High-Level Security ArchitectureTS 102 940Security Architecture & ManagementTS 102 941Trust & Privacy TS 102 942Access ControlTS 102 943ConfidentialityTS 103 097Security Header & Certificate Formats Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX System Architecture Description Version: V1.0 Authority) that has verified the certificate's contents (called the issuer of the certificate). If the signature is valid, and the software examining the certificate trusts the issuer, then it can use that public key to communicate securely with the certificate's subject. As part of a certificate enrolment procedure, a secret private key is generated together with the certificate which includes the public key. Figure 6.5-2 shows the PKI of the ETSI ITS security architecture. The PKI is comprised of the following entities: • Enrolment Authority (EA) In generic PKI terminology, an EA represents a Registration Authority (RA).

content.title: ConVeX ; deliverable D2.2: Deliverable D2.2: Final ConVeX system architecture description
content.abstract_or_snippet: the PC5 LTE sidelink interface, the PDCP layer provides functions for integrity and confidentiality protection [30], [31]. This includes functionality for key management Use of Transport Layer Security (TLS or DTLS) is currently not addressed in [12] and [24]. However, TLS/DTLS might be a reasonable choice for end-to-end security protection of communication when using the V2N/Uu interfaces, including V2N2V communication. Security Mechanisms Supported by the C-V2X Communication Platform This section briefly describes the security mechanisms planned to be supported by the C-V2X communication platform. CAM and DENM will comply with the ETSI ITS security header as defined in ETSI TS 103 097 [18] and described in Section 6.5.3.4 above. All C-V2X communication modules employed in the ConVeX test system will use pre- provisioned certificates. These certificates will be installed as part of the Application and ITS stack software installation procedure. At the present point in time, it is not decided whether or not functionality for remote re- enrolment/re-assignment of certificates will be provided. If such functionality is supported, it will likely not fully comply with ETSI PKI architecture as outlined in section 6.5.2 and instead be implemented in a more simple way where essentially all entities are combined on a single physical cloud server. Authentication and authorization of DENM and CAM will be performed based on the end-user identities associated with the deployed certificates. Integrity protection of messages will be applied using a suitable signature algorithm of the Digital Signature Standard recommended by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), e.g., ECDSA with nistp256 curve and SHA256 as recommended in ETSI TS 103 097 [19]. The exact details of the signature algorithms employed by the test system are to be defined. V2V and V2I (i.e., LTE sidelink/PC5) one-to-one unicast services supported by the ConVeX test system can be secured by using the security mechanisms specified in clause 6.5 of 3GPP 33.303 [31]. For unicast services requiring TCP/IP or UDP/IP a suitable choice for end-to-end security protection between ITS stations and the cloud and between ITS stations is to use certificate-based Transport Layer Security, using TLS 1.2 [28] or DTLS 1.2 [29]. content.title: KoMoD Schlussbericht : digitales Testfeld  Düsseldorf : 01.06.2017-31.08.2019
content.abstract_or_snippet: Förderkennzeichen 16AVF1006A 11. Seitenzahl 66 13. Literaturangaben - 14. Tabellen 8 15. Abbildungen 41 18. Kurzfassung Die Entwicklungen von Anwendungen des vernetzten und automatisierten Fahrens schreiten zügig voran. Viele Applikationen werden aber zunächst auf abgeschlossen Testfeldern erprobt. Die nächsten Entwicklungsschritte umfassend Testprogramme der verfügbaren Technologien in realen Verkehrssituationen. Zudem ist zu klären, inwieweit die bestehende Infrastruktur die Anwendungen unterstützen kann. Ziel des Forschungsvorhabens war die Bereitstellung eines Testfeldes zur Erprobung neuer Technologien der Fahrzeug- Infrastrukturvernetzung sowie des teil- und hochautomatisierten Fahrens unter realen Verkehrsbedingungen. Adressiert wurden insbesondere folgende Aufgabenbereiche:     Erprobung der Datenbereitstellung und Kommunikation. Analyse des Zusammenspiels von Informationsbereitstellung durch die Infrastruktur und Erfassung durch die bordautonome Sensorik im Fahrzeug. Analyse des Zusammenspiels der fahrzeugseitigen Systeme mit hochpräzisem Kartenmaterial unter Nutzung geeigneter Ortungstechnologien. Bewertung der umgesetzten Anwendungen zur Unterstützung von automatisierten Fahrfunktionen hinsichtlich der Auswirkungen auf Verkehrseffizienz, Verkehrssicherheit sowie auf Umwelteinträge. Zusammen mit Partnern aus der Verkehrstechnik und der Automobilzuliefererindustrie wurden im Testfeld V2X-Services bereitgestellt, die Fahrzeuge über Daten und Informationen mit der Infrastruktur vernetzen. Auf dieser Grundlage wurden umfangreiche Felderprobungen mit verschiedenen Versuchsträgerfahrzeugen durchgeführt. Diese wurden im Testfeld Düsseldorf auch verkehrsträgerübergreifend (MIV, ÖPNV und Radverkehr) aufgesetzt. Im Testbetrieb konnten wertvol

content.title: ACCorD - Korridor für neue Mobilität Aachen-Düsseldorf : Schlussbericht des Verbundes : Laufzeit des Vorhabens: von: 01.01.2020 (M1) bis: 31.03.2022 (M27)
content.abstract_or_snippet: modellen von Verkehrsteilnehmenden. Abschließend bietet die simulative Absicherung ein großes Potenzial, um neue Fahrfunktionen szenarienbasiert in der Simulation zu testen. Online können hingegen V2X-Nachrichten (CPMs) an automatisierte und vernetzte Forschungsfahrzeuge versendet werden, um die aktive Fahrfunktion durch externe Infrastrukturinformationen zu ergänzen und einen sicheren Fahrbetrieb zu gewährleisten. Die Verbreitung der V2X-Nachrichten kann dabei zentral über Mobilfunk (C-V2X) oder lokal über ITS-G5 RSU an den einzelnen Messstationen erfolgen. Die im Projekt errichtete Testumgebung soll auch nach Projektende für vollständig, das heißt nicht nur einzelne Testabschnitte, weiter für Forschungszwecke genutzt werden und der interessierten Öffentlichkeit zur Verfügung gestellt werden. Hier soll neben der Nutzung in ACCorD_Schlussbericht_v3.0, 31.05.2022 (M29) Datenfusion & TrackingTrajektorienSzenarienLive-Visualisierung & V2X⁞SzenarienextraktionDatenzugriffZentralerServer zurDatenverarbeitungund Administration FKZ 01MM19001A-G 42 öffentlich geförderten Forschungsprojekten auch die Möglichkeit einer Nutzung im Rahmen von Industrieforschung geprüft werden. Auf diese Weise könnte der Wirtschaftsstanddort Deutschland von der errichteten Testumgebung und dem entwickelten Know-how direkt profitieren, zielführend könnte dabei auch eine forcierte Nutzung durch KMUs sein. Wie im Projektantrag formuliert, könnte auf dies Weise auch die zum Weiterbetrieb erforderlichen Mittel erwirtschaftet werden. Außerdem wird so ein breiter Zugang für interessierte externe Nutzende geschaffen. Dabei ist sicherzustellen, dass die Nutzung für wissenschaftlichen Industrieforschung nachranging gegenüber der wissenschaftlichen hoheitlichen Nutzung erfolgen soll. Institut für Straßenwesen (isac) – RWTH Aachen University Automatisierte und vernetzte Fahrzeuge sind ein wichtiger Baustein zukünftiger Mobilitätssystem

updated_tasks.task_rationale: Liefert belastbare Definitionen und Taxonomie zur Beantwortung, was unter V2X zu verstehen ist.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Rolle/Funktionen von V2X über zentrale Use-Cases (Sicherheitswarnungen, kooperative Manöver, Signalphaseninfo) evidenzbasiert erfassen.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Zeigt konkret, welche Aufgaben V2X im Verkehrssystem übernimmt und welche Dienste dadurch ermöglicht werden.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Technologische Realisierungen vergleichen: ITS-G5/DSRC vs C-V2X/NR-V2X, Architektur und Kommunikationsmodelle.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Ordnet die Rolle von V2X in Kommunikationsstacks und Architekturen ein und erklärt, wie es technisch umgesetzt wird.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Regulatorische/standardisierende Einordnung in EU/DE: C-ITS, ETSI/3GPP/UNECE, Security/Credential-Management.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Konkretisiert die Rolle von V2X im rechtlich-normativen Rahmen (Interoperabilität, Sicherheit, Datenschutz).

updated_tasks.search_intent: Wirkungsnachweise: Studien zu Sicherheits- und Effizienzpotenzialen sowie Deployment-Hürden (Penetration, Kosten, Interoperabilität).
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Ergänzt die Rollenbeschreibung um belegte Nutzenpotenziale und Grenzen, wichtig für Einordnung und Bewertung.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: attributes as part of the V2V Service). The V2V payload must be flexible in order to accommodate different information contents. V2V is predominantly broadcast-based. Where a direct communication might not be possible e.g. due to the limited direct communication range, the V2V related application information exchange might be done using other V2X services alternatively, e.g. using additional infrastructure like RSU or application servers and the different underlying communication paths of V2I, V2N etc. potentially in a combined manner. Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) The V2I communication will also be implemented using LTE Sidelink Transmission Mode 4 on PC5, i.e. the RSU acts similar as a UE. Note that the 3GPP standard also foresees a variant of a V2I communication via Uu link, then assuming the RSU to be part of an eNode B, but this setup is again not used within this trial as it is no probable option for a future real world implementation. The UE supporting V2I applications transmits messages containing V2I application information to an RSU. The RSU and/or the locally relevant application server transmit messages containing V2I application information to one or more UEs supporting V2I applications. A locally relevant application server serves a particular geographic area. There can be multiple application servers serving overlapping areas, providing the same or different applications. Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P) V2P communication is very similar to V2V, also using PC5 LTE Sidelink Transmission Mode 4. The UE supporting V2P applications transmits application layer information. Such information can be broadcast by a vehicle with UE supporting V2X Service (e.g., warning to pedestrian), and/or by a pedestrian with UE supporting V2X Service (e.g., warning to vehicle). ConVeX Project Confidential 14 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1 Vehicle to Network (V2N) V2N is also introduced where one party is a UE and the other party is a serving entity, supporting both V2N applications and communicating with each other via cellular network (e.g. LTE or 5G). This means for the LTE case, the Uu interface will be used. Network to Vehicle/UE communication can be multicast or unicast. The serving entities are usually connected to public internet, and possibly to the backends of several

domain_applicability.reason: Beschreibt allgemein V2V/V2X Kommunikationsprinzipien (Broadcast, alternative Pfade via RSU/Server) ohne projektspezifische Detailabhängigkeit.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: 1 ConVeX High Level Network Architecture Communication connections in the ConVeX system architecture can be classified in the following way: Direct communication such as vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) is realized through PC5 (ProSe Communication reference point 5). The same holds true for vehicle-to-pedestrians (V2P) and vehicle- to-infrastructure (V2I). In addition the LTE-Uu connection complements any communication in the non-direct links within the (wide area) network. This connection will also be used as backhaul-link between road-side-units (RSU) and its (traffic management) center, acting as transparent IP connection. Figure 4.2-1 ConVeX High Level Network Architecture Types of V2X The vehicular communication referred to as Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X), contains following four different types:  Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communications ConVeX Project Confidential 13 (107) Deliverable D1.1: Use Cases, Requirements, Performance Evaluation Criteria Version: V1.1  Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) Communications  Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P) Communications  Vehicle-to-Network (V2N) Figure 4.2-1. illustrates these types of communications. Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) V2V communication is realized with the means of LTE V2V Sidelink on PC5 interface enabling a direct vehicle to vehicle communication. The LTE standard allows two different transmission modes on PC5: the so-called “in-coverage” mode, where an eNode B is scheduling the actual transmissions of the UE (Sidelink Transmission Mode 3), as well as the “out-of-coverage” mode where there is no eNode B scheduling involved, but an autonomous resource selection performed by the UEs (Sidelink Transmission Mode 4). The latter will be used for the trial and is also envisioned as the most relevant mode of operation for future real world usage. As mentioned above, the same PC5 communication will be used for V2I and V2P. The UE supporting V2V applications transmits application layer information (e.g. about its location, dynamics, and attributes as part of the V2V Service). The V2V payload must be flexible in order to accommodate different information contents. V2V is predominantly broadcast-based. Where a direct communication might not be possible e.g. due to the limited direct

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: Service: A type of V2X Service, where one party is a UE and the other party is an RSU both using V2I application. V2N Service: A type of V2X Service, where one party is a UE and the other party is a serving entity, both using V2N applications and communicating with each other via cellular network (e.g. LTE or 5G) . V2P Service: A type of V2X Service, where both parties of the communication are UEs using V2P application. For this service the vehicle-side UE is a V-ITS-S, the pedestrian-side (respectively VRU-side) UE is a P-ITS-S. V2V Service: A type of V2X Service, where both parties of the communication are UEs using V2V application. For this service both vehicle-side UEs represent V-ITS-S. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: und B übergreifende Themen unterstütze ZF während der gesamten Projektlaufzeit. Randbedingungen Eine wesentliche Randbedingung beim Datenaustausch mit den Fahrzeugen war und ist das Thema Datenschutz. Hier wurde durch geeignete Voraggregation und Anonymisierung sichergestellt, dass die Vorgaben der EU DSGVO eingehalten werden. Versuchsträger des Instituts für Kraftfahrzeuge der RWTH Aachen Im Rahmen des Projekts wurde in Abstimmung mit den Projektpartnern eine generische und Use Case übergreifende Kommunikationsschnittstelle entwickelt, welche sowohl über ITS-G5 (Nahfeldkommunikation/WLAN 802.11p), als auch Mobilfunk (LTE/4G), parallel Informationen austauschen kann. Hierbei kommen für die beiden Kommunikationswege unterschiedliche Module zum Einsatz. Für die Nahfeldkommunikation wird eine Cohda Wireless MK5 Onbord Unit (OBU) verwendet. Diese ist aus dem Vorgängerprojekt KoMoD verfügbar und wurde um eine PKI- Integration (Public Key Infrastructure) erweitert, um eine Signierung von V2X-Nachrichten zu ermöglichen. Die Mobilfunkkommunikation erfolgt hingegen über einen, von Vodafone bereitgestellten V2X-Connector. Die Software der beiden Module ist dabei so gestaltet, dass die erzeugten Nachrichten im gleichen Format von beiden Modulen verarbeitet und weitergeleitet werden können. Die Module erwarten dabei eine ASN.1-Kodierung der Nachrichten, die von entsprechenden Verarbeitungsalgorithmen vorgenommen werden muss. Neben der Kommunikations-Hardware ist auch die Definition der auszutauschenden Nachrichtentypen eine relevante Grundlage für die Charakterisierung der V2X-Schnittstelle. Dabei wurden die von der ETSI standardisierten Nachrichtentypen verwendet. Relevant sind vor allem die folgenden Nachrichtentypen:  DENM  CAM  MAP  SPAT  IVI  CPM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message) (Cooperative Awareness Message) (MapData Messages) (Signal Phase And Timing Messages) (Infrastructure to Vehicle Information) (Collective Perception Message) Um die,

domain_applicability.reason: Beschreibt allgemein Randbedingungen wie DSGVO, Voraggregation und Anonymisierung beim Fahrzeugdatenaustausch; keine starken Systemspezifika.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: die Werte für die erlaubte Latenz bei maximal 10ms bis 500ms und für die Reliabilität oberhalb der Werte zwischen 90% und 99.999%. 11 SMART Projekt 2 Grundlagen 2.3.2 Typen und Inhalte von V2X-Nachrichten Der Standard „Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)“ [3] sieht zur Koordination autonom fahrender Fahrzeuge einen periodischen Nachrichtenaustausch zwischen den Fahrzeugen vor. Hierbei wird prinzipiell zwischen zwei Nachrichtentypen unterschieden. Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM) Das sind periodisch gesendete Nachrichten, die Informationen über Position, Bewegung, Fahrzeu- geigenschaften enthalten. [4] Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages (DENM) Diese Nachrichten werden durch (sicherheitskritische) Verkehrssituationen ausgelöste, die bei- spielsweise eine Notbremsung oder einen Überholvorgang einleiten. Die Nachrichten werden, so- lange die Situation besteht, periodisch wiederholt. [5] Zusätzlich sind in [2] weitere Nachrichten definiert, die in ähnlichen Formaten wie CAM und/oder DENM ausgetauscht werden. Hierbei unterscheiden sich Nachrichteninhalt, Paketgrößen und Dienstgüteanforderungen abhängig vom angestrebten Automatisierungsgrad und der V2X-Anwen- dung. Es sind folgende Nachrichteninhalte definiert. Wahrnehmungsdaten Das sind Informationen, die den Wahrnehmungsbereich eines Fahrzeuges erweitern. Mögliche Nachrichteninhalte sind vorverarbeiteten Sensordaten, die abstrakte Informationen über detektierte Objekt enthalten, und hochauflösenden Wahrnehmungsdaten wie Sensorrohdaten einer Kamera oder eines Light Detection and Ranging (LIDAR) Systems. Fahrabsichten Informationen zur Koordination von Fahrmanövern. Es wird zwischen groben Ereignissen wie der Absicht zum Spurwechsel oder Anhalten zu definierbaren Zeitpunkten und Positionen und detail- lierten Trajektorien unterschieden. Kartendaten Nachrichteninhalt sind hochauflösende Kartendaten, die periodisch in

domain_applicability.reason: Allgemeine V2X-Grundlagen zu Latenz/Reliabilität und CAM als Nachrichtentyp im ITS-Standard; gut übertragbar für Road-V2X.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: for this but relies on the 3GPP interfaces in a hybrid fashion. There are some commonalities but as well as some fundamental differences between the 3GPP PC5 based C-V2X and the ETSI ITS-G5 V2X approaches. The main difference exists in the lower layers of MAC and PHY, and the integration within the whole protocol stack up to the service/application layer mainly with respect to management and security. Section 5 is providing these insights and comparison in more detail. ConVeX Project 16 (77) Deliverable D3.1: Comparative Analysis of Candidate V2X Radio Technologies V1.0 Analysis of V2X Technologies Introduction The present section 5 describes the main functions and characteristics of the current candidate V2X radio technologies: • DSRC/WAVE specified by the US Department of Transportation (U.S. DOT) • ETSI C-ITS / ITS-G5 specified by ETSI by mandate of the European Commission • 3GPP Rel-14 Cellular V2X based on LTE Uu and PC5 radio technologies Both DSRC/WAVE and ETSI C-ITS / ITS-G5 are based on IEEE 802.11 WLAN radio technologies, namely a MAC/PHY variant of the 802.11a standard which is denoted IEEE 802.11p. Therefore, both technologies are colloquially often referred to as “WLAN-based” or “WiFi-based” V2X technologies. Section 5.2 introduces the overall protocol stacks of DSRC/WAVE and ETSI C- ITS / ITS-G5 and elaborates the commonalities and differences between these technologies. Section 5.3 presents the main features of 3GPP Rel-14 LTE-based cellular V2X radio technologies examined in the ConVeX project, namely LTE Uu radio technology applied for V2N communications, and LTE PC5 (“sidelink”) radio technology for V2V/V2I/V2P communications. Section 5.4 provides an analysis of the main performance characteristics of the considered V2X technologies. 802.11p-based Technologies Introduction to 802.11p, DSRC and ITS-G5 The IEEE 802.11p specification has been developed as an amendment [31] to the IEEE 802.11 WLAN standard series of specifications. The goal of this effort was to introduce radio technology enhancements in order to support communication between vehicles (V2V) and between vehicle and road side infrastructure
